concepts.title: Peasants and Workers Party of India (PWPI) and National Workers Party (NWP)
concepts.description: The PWPI and NWP were the first to raise the slogan of 'No Taxes' in 1936.

concepts.title: Indian National Congress's role in the rise of popular movements
concepts.description: The Indian National Congress played a crucial role in the rise of popular movements by launching the Civil Disobedience Movement, but also failed to take a clear stand on the peasant and worker movements.

concepts.title: Key Features of the Civil Disobedience Movement
concepts.description: The key features of the Civil Disobedience Movement were the use of non-violent resistance, mass mobilization, and the defiance of British laws and regulations.

concepts.title: M.N. Roy's views on the peasant and worker movements
concepts.description: M.N. Roy's views can be remembered using the acronym 'RISE': R- Rural Revolution, I- Industrial Action, S- Social Change, E- Economic Development.

concepts.title: Confusion between the Civil Disobedience Movement and the Non-Cooperation Movement
concepts.description: Students often confuse the Civil Disobedience Movement with the Non-Cooperation Movement. However, the key difference is that the Civil Disobedience Movement was launched by Mahatma Gandhi in 1930, while the Non-Cooperation Movement was launched by Mahatma Gandhi in 1920.

concepts.title: Key figures in the peasant and worker movements
concepts.description: Key figures in the peasant and worker movements include M.N. Roy, S.A. Dange, and B.C. Pal, who advocated for a rural revolution, industrial action, and social change.

concepts.title: The significance of the Civil Disobedience Movement in the rise of popular movements
concepts.description: The Civil Disobedience Movement marked a significant shift in the Indian independence movement, as it emphasized non-violent resistance and mass mobilization.

concepts.title: The role of the Communist Party of India in the rise of popular movements
concepts.description: The Communist Party of India played a crucial role in the rise of popular movements by advocating for a socialist revolution and supporting the peasant and worker movements.
